title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Inclusion of Arts in the STEM Curriculum (STEAM)

texts.A: The well-intentioned push to transform STEM into STEAM by formally incorporating 'Arts' into the curriculum is a misguided attempt to soften disciplines that derive their value precisely from their rigour and commitment to objective truth. Science and mathematics require a level of precision, verifiable accuracy, and logical coherence that is fundamentally different in character from the necessarily subjective and interpretive nature of artistic expression. These are not simply different subjects; they represent genuinely different epistemological approaches to knowledge. [37]By unnecessarily diluting the science and mathematics curriculum with artistic activities, we risk falling measurably and perhaps irreversibly behind in the intensely competitive global race for technological innovation and scientific discovery.[/37] The arts are absolutely vital to a civilized, humane, and culturally rich society, but they should be actively pursued as a meaningful complement to a rigorous technical education, not integrated into it in a manner that distracts students from the core analytical competencies employers urgently need.
texts.B: A careful examination of history reveals that the most significant and lasting breakthroughs in both science and the arts have consistently come from those rare individuals capable of bridging the analytical and the creative with equal fluency and depth. [38]Design thinking—an inherently aesthetic and artistic process of iterative problem-solving—is precisely what transforms a merely functional piece of technology into a genuinely revolutionary and widely adopted product that changes behaviour.[/38] I broadly share the view that our current educational culture over-prioritizes rote memorization and algorithmic procedure in science at the expense of imagination and genuine curiosity. [40]By meaningfully integrating arts education and creative practice, we teach students the crucial ability to visualize complex and abstract data and to think genuinely 'outside the box' of established convention.[/40] [39]The artificial and long-standing divide between C.P. Snow's famous 'two cultures' is a twentieth-century relic that is actively hindering twenty-first-century progress in ways we are only beginning to quantify.[/39]
texts.C: [38]I agree that the meaningful integration of arts and creative thinking can genuinely foster innovation and lateral problem-solving across disciplines, but I share the deep concern that the fashionable label 'STEAM' has in practice become a convenient buzzword routinely used by administrators to justify damaging budget cuts to dedicated and specialist arts programs.[/38] If the only institutional justification permitted for saving music education or fine art is demonstrating that it helps students build better engineering prototypes, then we have already fundamentally lost the battle for the intrinsic value of culture. [40]Arts must be genuinely valued for their own sake and for their unique contribution to human meaning, not merely as a utilitarian 'servant' of the sciences.[/40] However, [37]I fundamentally disagree with the premise that the arts are somehow inherently 'less rigorous' than science; achieving genuine mastery of a musical instrument or attaining real skill in fine art requires as much sustained discipline, deliberate practice, and intellectual commitment as any advanced calculus course.[/37]
texts.D: The entire debate over STEAM fundamentally misses a crucial and urgent point about the future of work: the labour market of the near future will automate the most purely 'technical' and rule-based skills first, precisely because these are the most straightforward to replicate algorithmically and at scale. [39]What genuinely cannot be easily automated in the foreseeable future is the deeply human capacity for empathy, nuanced ethical judgment, and the ability to construct and communicate meaningful narrative—qualities that the humanities and arts uniquely and systematically develop.[/39] While I share the skepticism that casually adding a weekly drawing session to a physics class constitutes any kind of meaningful 'solution' to our educational challenges, I believe a far more radical and comprehensive overhaul of the entire educational paradigm is urgently needed. We should not simply append an 'A' to STEM; we should fundamentally rebuild our entire curriculum around the deeply human capabilities that science and technology alone are structurally incapable of providing.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from A on whether the arts can be integrated with science without losing rigor?
questions.explanation: Expert A argues that arts 'dilute' the curriculum and distract from core competencies. Expert C argues that arts are not 'less rigorous' and require as much discipline as calculus, supporting their value in education.

questions.question: Which expert shares B's opinion about the role of creativity in technological breakthroughs?
questions.explanation: Expert B says design thinking turns technology into 'revolutionary products.' Expert C agrees that 'integration of arts can foster innovation.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the limitations of a purely technical education for the future job market?
questions.explanation: Expert D says 'pure' technical skills will be automated and we need 'human' skills. Expert B also argues that the 'artificial divide' between cultures is hindering progress in the 21st century.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the primary reason for including arts in the curriculum?
questions.explanation: Expert C believes arts should be valued 'for their own sake' and not as a servant to science. Expert B argues for arts inclusion primarily because it improves scientific and technological results (visualization, thinking outside the box).
